etymology_and_history.root_language: Language**: From "hot" + "tempered" from Old English "hāt" + "temprian" (heated disposition)
etymology_and_history.original_meaning: Meaning**: Heated disposition; experiencing quick anger and hot-tempered quickness supported by coolness awareness
etymology_and_history.semantic_evolution: Evolution**: Heated disposition → quick anger → contemporary hot-tempered quickness and hot-tempered anger that benefits from serenity growth

contemporary_usage.register: Negative concern; indicates quick anger calling for coolness support and serenity development
